original_hypothesis.title: Unauthorized proving pause: TaikoL1.pauseProving() can be called by anyone (missing access control on pause/unpause)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.pauseProving(bool) calls _authorizePause(msg.sender) but does not apply any modifier. However, TaikoL1 overrides _authorizePause(address) with a modifier-only signature and an empty body, meaning the modifier may never execute when called as an internal function. This can make pauseProving callable by any external account, allowing unprivileged attackers to permanently stall proving/verifying (proveBlock/verifyBlocks are gated by whenProvingNotPaused).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a subtle Solidity pattern: applying modifiers to internal functions only has effect when the function is invoked as a function call; if overridden and called from a base, the compiled code may not execute the overridden version/modifier as intended. If access control is bypassed, a single tx can pause proving and halt the rollup’s finality pipeline.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pauseProving() directly calls _authorizePause(msg.sender); TaikoL1 overrides _authorizePause(address) with `override onlyFromOwnerOrNamed("chain_pauser") { }` (empty body). Potential modifier-not-applied / override dispatch ambiguity.

preconditions: Overridden _authorizePause() modifier is not effectively enforced at runtime (to be validated by compilation/bytecode inspection).
Proving pause impacts liveness because proveBlock/verifyBlocks require whenProvingNotPaused.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: access control delegated to an internal call to an overridden function whose body is empty and only has a modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: modifier may not execute as intended; pauseProving becomes permissionless
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit onlyOwner/onlyFromOwnerOrNamed modifier on pauseProving() itself

analysis_questions: Does TaikoL1.pauseProving() enforce onlyFromOwnerOrNamed(chain_pauser) at runtime?
Is _authorizePause resolved statically to EssentialContract._authorizePause (onlyOwner) or dynamically to TaikoL1 override?
What does compiled bytecode for pauseProving() contain (does it inline a resolver check)?
validation_requirements: Compile and inspect TaikoL1 bytecode / run Foundry test: unprivileged caller calls pauseProving(true) and observe success/revert
Check Solidity dispatch rules for internal virtual function override + modifier-only override
If vulnerable: craft PoC showing unprivileged global proving DoS

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls TaikoL1.pauseProving(true). If succeeds, all proveBlock/verifyBlocks revert due to whenProvingNotPaused, halting finalization until a privileged party unpauses.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High systemic impact: chain stalls; bond capital locked; bridging relying on state root sync may halt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users/provers/bridges depending on L1 verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires privileged intervention (if attacker can also unpause repeatedly, can sustain DoS)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No explicit test shown asserting pauseProving reverts for unprivileged callers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: on-chain events ProvingPaused(bool) emitted; easy to monitor

validation_result.mechanism: Access control via internal function modifier
validation_result.impact_summary: The hypothesis claims unauthorized users can pause proving, but the code correctly enforces access control, preventing this.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis asserts that modifiers on internal functions might not execute when called, specifically pointing to `_authorizePause` in `TaikoL1`. However, this is based on a misunderstanding of Solidity's mechanics. Solidity fully supports modifiers on internal functions. When `pauseProving` calls `_authorizePause(msg.sender)`, the `onlyFromOwnerOrNamed` modifier is executed as part of the function call. Since `msg.sender` is preserved in internal calls, the modifier correctly checks if the caller is the owner or the 'chain_pauser' and reverts if they are not. There is no bypass, and the access control is strictly enforced.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The `pauseProving` function directly calls `_authorizePause(msg.sender)`. The `_authorizePause` function in `TaikoL1` is defined with the `onlyFromOwnerOrNamed("chain_pauser")` modifier. The modifier explicitly checks `msg.sender != owner() && msg.sender != resolve(_name, true)` and reverts if the condition is met. This guarantees that only authorized accounts can execute the function.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The execution path correctly routes through the access control modifier, which reverts the transaction for unauthorized callers.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker calling `TaikoL1.pauseProving(true)` will trigger the `_authorizePause` internal call. The `onlyFromOwnerOrNamed` modifier will evaluate `msg.sender`. Since the attacker is neither the owner nor the resolved 'chain_pauser', the modifier will revert the transaction with `RESOLVER_DENIED()`. The attack scenario is impossible.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The vulnerability does not exist.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy TaikoL1, call `pauseProving(true)` from an account that is not the owner and not the 'chain_pauser'. Observe that the transaction reverts with `RESOLVER_DENIED()`.
